$comment: Hand-authored taxonomy. Powers the Topic Front Door (/t, /t/:topic) and the section list inside each state hub (/s/:state). See ADR-0022 and TODO/IA-RESET-PLACE-FIRST-WITH-TOPIC-FRONT-DOOR.md. DOCTRINE (user-mandated 2026-05-11): elections are ONE indicator family among many. Social-welfare topics (fiscal, education, health, livelihood) are first-class; elections are not the spine and not the default theme. Order in this array IS the rendering order on /t and inside the state hub.

topics.title: Fiscal capacity
topics.summary: How states raise, borrow, and spend. Debt, deficit, transfers from the Centre. The baseline that contextualises every other state-level number.
topics.notes: Debt and deficit are co-determined by central transfers — the transfers indicators (RBI Net via `fiscal/net_transfers_from_centre`, Rajya Sabha Gross via `fiscal/centre_transfers_gross`) are the contextualisation that makes ranked debt/deficit tables honest. Per ADR-0022 ingest gate. RBI Net covers FY24 Accounts + FY25 RE + FY26 BE; Rajya Sabha Gross covers FY17–FY23 (longer historical run, devolution + grants pre-adjustments). The all-India aggregate equivalent (RBI Net Centre→States transfers, FY08–FY26, 19y) is `fiscal/centre_transfers_to_states_net` — a country-entity series for the macro envelope view; its data shape is incompatible with the per-state choropleth (it carries one row per FY for `entity_id="IN"`). The four `fiscal/states_combined_*_deficit` indicators (gross fiscal, revenue, primary, primary revenue) come from RBI Appendix Table 1 (Major Deficit Indicators) — same publication as the transfers, all-states aggregate, FY08–FY26 (20y including RE/BE). They answer the macro question 'how much are the states collectively borrowing each year, and what kind of borrowing is it?'. The matching `fiscal/union_*_deficit` quartet (RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy, Table 89, FY87–FY26 / 40y) ships the Centre's own borrowing as a peer family — without it, ranked deficit tables silently misframe responsibility (cf. Hans's chart-trap warning in docs/concepts/fiscal-actor-naming.md: states-combined GFD ~3% of GDP looks alarming until placed beside the Centre's ~6%). The seven per-state component indicators (`state_own_tax_revenue`, `state_non_tax_revenue`, `state_share_central_taxes`, `state_grants_in_aid`, `state_revenue_expenditure`, `state_pension_expenditure`, `state_external_debt`) are absolute ₹Cr — large states will dominate the table by sheer size. The renderer's existing comparability banner ('Per-capita / per-area normalisation is recommended for cross-state comparison') surfaces this; treat the rankings as 'how big is this stream' not 'how well is the state managing'. The own-tax-revenue / GSDP and pension / revenue-expenditure derived ratios (Phase 5) are the citizen-facing performance reads — these absolute components ship now so the build pipeline + drift detector cover them and so the per-state hubs can show them in context.

topics.title: Energy
topics.summary: Where electricity is generated. Installed capacity by state, by fuel.
topics.notes: Installed-MW is a *siting* statistic, not a consumption statistic — the state where a plant sits is not necessarily the state that consumes the power. The indicator artifact's `attribution_geography` field carries this caveat to the renderer. The `installed_capacity_*` family is from CEA's monthly Executive Summary (March 2026 snapshot) and covers all 35 CEA-reported state/UT entities across 7 fuel categories (total, thermal, coal, gas, nuclear, hydro, renewable). The legacy `installed_mw_by_state` indicator (Wikipedia-derived, 4 states only) is retained for back-compat but no longer the default. The state-level family (`state_installed_capacity_*`, `state_electricity_*`, `state_rooftop_solar_capacity_mw`, `state_atc_losses_pct`, `state_acs_arr_gap_inr_per_kwh`) is from the NITI Aayog ICED state-wise deep-dive API and covers 9-11 fiscal years (FY16-FY26) per indicator across all 36 states/UTs plus the All-India aggregate. ICED publishes both 'geographical-location' (where the plant sits) and 'with-allocated-shares' (who has rights to the output) capacity series; we ship both so the analyst can pick the one that matches the question being asked. The 2026-05-15 ICED-power batch adds five complementary series from a different ICED endpoint family (NITI ICED public dashboards, not the state-wise deep-dive): `state_installed_capacity_by_source_mw` (FY16-FY26, 11y, all states × ~7 fuels, 1815 rows — same metric as the state-wise `state_installed_capacity_geographical_mw` but tidy-by-fuel and with longer history), `state_electricity_generation_by_source_gwh` and `state_peak_electricity_demand_mw` (FY26 snapshot from `/energy/powerStatistics`, useful as a current-year companion to the multi-year capacity series), and two national series — `india_thermal_capacity_retired_mw` (FY06-FY26, by retired-fuel facet) and `india_capacity_pipeline_gw` (2011-2031, two pipeline-status facets, including forward-looking commissioning estimates). Pipeline is the only forward-looking energy series we ship and is explicitly flagged as projection in `series_breaks`. The 2026-05-15 ICED-power state-extension batch adds eight more state-scope series from the same NITI ICED public dashboards: `state_installed_capacity_total_mw` (state totals across all fuels), the `peak_demand_mw` / `peak_met_mw` pair (peak load served vs requested — the gap is the operational shortfall, but it ships as two raw series rather than a derived ratio so the citizen can see both numbers), the `power_requirement_mu` / `power_availability_mu` pair (annual energy-volume equivalent of peak — a fuller-year shortfall view), `per_capita_availability_kwh` and `per_capita_electricity_consumption_kwh` (citizen-experience metrics already normalised by population so cross-state comparison is honest — the latter is `comparable_across_states` and the headline citizen read of 'how electrified is daily life here'), and `renewable_grid_capacity_mw` (grid-connected renewables only, complements the existing `state_rooftop_solar_capacity_mw`). The two pairs (peak demand/met, requirement/availability) are intentionally shipped as raw series rather than as derived shortfall % — the renderer's existing comparability banner surfaces normalisation guidance, and the explicit pairing in topic notes here lets the citizen reason about gaps without us pre-committing to one ratio framing. Three remaining national-entity series (`national_final_energy_consumption_by_sector_mtoe`, `national_primary_energy_supply_mtoe`, `national_renewable_potential_vs_installed_mw`) stay allowlisted pending Phase 4 country-entity renderers — they are multi-facet and the IndicatorChoropleth state-only renderer cannot show them honestly.

topics.title: Elections
topics.summary: Who governs. State Assembly and Lok Sabha results, candidate-level detail, party totals, margins. One indicator family among many — not the spine of the site.
topics.notes: Per ADR-0022 doctrine: elections are NOT the default home-page theme and NOT a featured topic. Social-welfare topics (fiscal, future: education, health, livelihood) are first-class; elections are one of many. Election artifacts have a different schema family (result.summary.schema.json) than indicators — the catalogue's polymorphic `kind` lets them coexist without being demoted in capability or promoted in IA prominence.

topics.title: Economy
topics.summary: How big is each state's economy, in real and nominal terms. GDP and sectoral GVA at constant and current prices, in Lakh Crore Rupees.
topics.notes: Source: NITI Aayog ICED state-wise deep-dive API (which republishes MoSPI National Statistical Office figures with the 2011-12 base year). Covers 10 fiscal years (FY16-FY25) for all 36 states/UTs plus All-India. Constant-price series strips inflation; current-price series is the nominal 'sticker' value. Compare GDP vs Sectoral GVA: GDP includes net product taxes; GVA is the cleaner production-side measure. The 2025-26 row is typically N.A. while NSO finalises that year's accounts. The per-capita NSDP and per-capita consumption indicators come from the ICED key-economic-indicators API (a separate endpoint family from state-wise deep-dive); they go back to 2004-05 and 2009-10 respectively, longer than the GDP series, because the source publisher provides a longer back-run of those particular metrics. The four `state_nsdp_*` and `state_per_capita_nsdp_*_long` indicators are RBI Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy splices: `state_nsdp_constant_inr_crore` and `state_nsdp_current_inr_crore` are absolute ₹Cr (large states will dominate the table by sheer size — the existing comparability banner advises per-capita normalisation), while the `_long` per-capita variants extend FY81→FY26 by stitching across 1980-81, 1999-2000, 2004-05 and 2011-12 base-year breaks. SeriesBreakAnnotation (Phase 2) renders these breaks visibly so a citizen reading 'India's per-capita NSDP grew Nx since 1980' knows the slope crosses methodology revisions and is not a single coherent measurement. The legacy `state_per_capita_nsdp_current_inr` (non-`_long`) is permanently allowlisted as a diff-check artifact only — superseded by the spliced version. Topic is featured=false pending IA review (the doctrine says social-welfare topics drive IA; pure macro economy is context, not headline).

topics.title: Demography
topics.summary: How many people live where. State population (inter-censal estimates) until the next decadal Census resets the baseline.
topics.notes: Two complementary population series. `state_population_lakhs` is the FY16-FY26 inter-censal projection used for per-capita normalisations on other indicators (denominator role). `state_population_by_sex_count` is the decennial Census enumeration (1961-2011) faceted by sex, useful for sex-ratio analysis and historical context. The most recent Census of India was 2011; the 2021 round was deferred. Topic is featured=false pending the next Census release; until then this is mostly the denominator for per-capita normalisations rather than a headline series.

topics.title: Human development
topics.summary: How states score on the composite of income, health and education — what an average resident's life chances look like, beyond pure income.
topics.notes: Source: NITI Aayog ICED HDI map (sub-national HDI computed by NITI from Indian survey inputs — NFHS for health, NSS/PLFS for education and income). Not directly comparable to UNDP's national HDI for India: input series differ. ICED publishes only two snapshot years (2011-12 and 2017-18) — treat this as a position, not a trajectory. We do not draw a trend line through two points.

topics.title: Environment
topics.summary: What India emits and what burden it puts on the climate system. National greenhouse-gas inventory broken down by sector.
topics.notes: National total only — sub-national emissions accounting does not yet exist for India. Reported in India's Biennial Update Report (BUR) submissions to UNFCCC, methodology per IPCC 2006 guidelines. LULUCF (land-use / land-use-change / forestry) shows up as a net-negative sector when forest absorption exceeds deforestation; that is real, not an error. Per-capita Indian emissions are roughly a quarter of the OECD average; absolute totals reflect a population of 1.4 billion. State-level breakdown remains an open research problem (no government series exists yet). The sub-sector drill-down (`india_ghg_emissions_by_subsector_ggco2e`) is the same UNFCCC source one level deeper — Energy splits into Energy Industries, Transport, Manufacturing, Other Sectors, Fugitive emissions; Agriculture splits into Rice Cultivation, Enteric Fermentation, Manure Management, Agricultural Soils, Field Burning of Residues; etc. Sub-sector totals reconcile to the parent sector indicator when summed within each sector.

topics.title: Prices and inflation
topics.summary: What things cost, and how fast those prices are changing. Monetary policy is set by the Reserve Bank of India under a Union List mandate to keep headline inflation between 2% and 6% — states do not control inflation directly, so a higher state CPI usually reflects commodity composition, monsoon, and global oil prices, not state-government performance.
topics.notes: Three official price-series families coexist in India and each measures a different basket. CPI-Combined (Rural+Urban, base 2012=100) is the RBI's official target — the headline you read in news. CPI-IW (Industrial Workers, base 2016=100) is older and used to index dearness allowance for central-government employees and many wage settlements; useful for long history. WPI (Wholesale Price Index, base 2011-12=100) measures producer-stage prices and is what GDP deflators are built on; it is NOT a cost-of-living index. State CPI inflation is decomposed by NSO from the same all-India CPI sampling frame; differences across states reflect commodity weights and local price formation, not state-government policy. Index series get a Rebase Banner above the chart so the citizen reads levels as 'percent of base year' not 'rupees', and year-on-year is the comparable read. Per Hans's framing review.

topics.title: Transport — EV adoption
topics.summary: How the Indian vehicle fleet is shifting to electric. Roads and motor-vehicle taxation are State List subjects but the EV push is centrally driven via FAME-II subsidies and PM E-Drive — adoption is the joint outcome of state road-tax waivers, charging infrastructure, and citizen affordability.
topics.notes: Source: VAHAN dashboard (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways). Counts are absolute registrations and reflect both fleet size (large states register more vehicles in absolute terms regardless of EV share) and the EV share of new registrations. Read the share before the count. Telangana was carved out of Andhra Pradesh in 2014 — pre-2014 series for both states are partial. Goa-style small states routinely top the share table because their absolute base is small.

topics.title: Public health
topics.summary: Vital rates and public spending on health. Public health is a State List subject (Entry 6) — state governments run primary health centres, district hospitals, and most preventive programmes — but the Centre funds about a third of the spend through centrally-sponsored schemes (NHM, Ayushman Bharat).
topics.notes: Vital rates from the Sample Registration System (SRS) — the official continuous demographic survey run by the Office of the Registrar General of India. SRS publishes annual state-level estimates roughly two years in arrears. Crude birth and death rates are population-weighted — they fall partly because states age and birth-cohorts shrink, not only because of public-health gains; read alongside TFR. IMR (Infant Mortality Rate, deaths under 1 per 1,000 live births) is the cleanest health-system indicator: lower is better, and small states with strong primary care (Kerala, Tamil Nadu) sit near OECD lows while large EAG states still post double-digit rates. Public health expenditure ships in absolute ₹Crore — the per-capita view (Phase 5 derived) is the honest comparison; large states will dominate the absolute table but spend less per capita.
